title: Letters to the Editor
summary: GAS PRICES PINCH RVERS SAY FUEL COSTS CUT OTHER SPENDING By Mike Hinman Daily News Reporter (Published June 22, 2000) Not everyone in Alaska is celebrating high oil prices. John Medoro, a retired engineer from Colorado Springs, Colo., can pay as much as $400 to fill the fuel tank of his mammoth RV. His converted motor coach needs to be refueled only every 1,000 miles or so: It stands 14 feet high, is 40 feet long and totes a 260-gallon diesel tank. But at four miles to the gallon, he'd rather not think about filling up - he just sends his wife in to pay. Gale Harroff writes in his diary while his wife, Barbara, pages through a visitors guide at Anchorage RV Park. Harroff said high gas prices haven't slowed his travel plans, which include spending a couple of weeks in town. "At first you gr…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: DON'T GET INVOLVED IN LIVES SUPERVISORS SHOULD STICK TO JOB ISSUES By Lynne Curry (Published June 26, 2000) Question: Two months ago, two of my employees became roommates. This arrangement seems to be affecting their work. They've become very involved in each other's personal lives and one of them seems quite preoccupied with the personal problems the other has. What do you suggest I do before things go further downhill? My common sense says I should talk to both employees together because they both had a history of good work before they started living together. Answer: Your common sense may get you into a great deal of trouble. Supervisors can't afford to step over the line between supervisor and personal life adviser. As a supervisor you have the right to counsel your employees about any…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: 2 MEN LOST ON RIVERS By Larry Campbell Daily News Reporter (Published June 26, 2000) One man died and another was presumed dead Sunday after separate river accidents south of Anchorage, Alaska State Troopers said. A rafting accident killed a man late Sunday afternoon at Sixmile River along the Hope Highway on the south side of Turnagain Arm. Troopers said David Worman, 40, of Kenai, died after the raft he was riding capsized on the river during a run by Nova River Runners. The accident occurred just after 5 p.m. Troopers spokesman Greg Wilkinson said three rafts were on the river when two overturned, one with six people aboard and the other with five. Raft guides brought Worman and a woman to shore, but Worman was not breathing. A helicopter took Worman to Providence Alaska Medical Center,…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: EPA CITES FOUR SHIPS AIR POLLUTION ALLEGED By Martha Bellisle Daily News Juneau Bureau (Published June 22, 2000) Smoke spewing from the stacks of four cruise ships docked here recently appeared to be in violation of federal and state air pollution limits, a federal regulator who conducted the surprise inspections said Wednesday. "Each ship was informed that their smoke was in excess of what is allowed by the state air quality (law) and the federal Clean Air Act," said John Pavitt, Alaska air quality coordinator for the federal Environmental Protection Agency. Results from June 10-12 inspections will be reviewed by the agency's Seattle regional office, which will determine whether air pollution violations occurred and whether to issue citations, Pavitt said. The flourishing cruise ship indu…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: BUSINESS PEOPLE (Published June 26, 2000) The Alaska Native Health Board announced three staff changes. Lureta Whitewing-Porter was welcomed back to the HIV/AIDS prevention program as the training specialist. She has more than 17 years' experience in education, counseling, prevention and health promotion. Elizabeth LeBlanc was named to the Rural Alaska Sanitation Coalition and Alaska Solid Waste Management Program as the project manager. LeBlanc has more than 18 years' experience in land management, land use, community planning and project management. Nicholas Morgan was promoted to technical support specialist for the RASC/SWM program. Morgan will provide support to all activities of RASC/SWM programs. Morgan has more than eight years' experience in the health prevention/promotion field. …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: ALASKA AIRLINES' CHIEF OF MAINTENANCE RETIRES The Associated Press TY: Wire (Published June 25, 2000) Alaska Airlines' top maintenance official has taken early retirement as the carrier grapples with criticism and a criminal investigation of its maintenance operations that intensified after the January crash of Flight 261. John Fowler, 52, executive vice president for technical operations and systems control, announced his decision Friday. "The past five months have been difficult for our company and all of our employees," Fowler wrote, according to Alaska officials who posted the message on the company's employee Web site. He said he had concluded that "a change needs to occur in order for this great company to turn the corner and move forward" and that his resignation would help. "Alaska…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: BARROW RESIDENTS APPLAUD AS THUNDER CLAPS By Earl Finkler (Published June 21, 2000) In the home of whale hunts and polar bears, where the sun shines all day in summer, and not at all in winter, people saw something that many had never seen here before: a thunderstorm. Meteorologist Chuck Evans was having a quiet night at the National Weather Service office downtown Monday. "Then I heard this thunder," he said, "and the phone started ringing off the hook. Everyone wanted to know what was going on." What was going on was a full-blown thunder and lightning storm passing through Barrow between 11 p.m. and midnight. The storm had residents in awe, scared local dogs and dumped nearly two-hundredths of an inch of rain, a torrent for these parts, where only 0.029 inches fell all of last month. The…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: COPS CATCH BANK SUSPECTS SHOTS FIRED DURING WILD ROAD CHASE By Larry Campbell And Molly Brown (Published June 28, 2000) Two gunmen botched a robbery of a South Anchorage credit union early Tuesday morning when police got on their tail and followed them on a wild, gun-shooting chase through the Dimond Boulevard and Old Seward Highway shopping district. Cops and robbers provided a dramatic live show for shoppers and tourists that ended with two men in custody. Police identified the suspects as Raymond L. Hubbard II, 21, and William E. Piers, 25. Anchorage police and FBI agents were questioning both in relation to numerous charges, including bank robbery and attempted murder. Events began just after 8 a.m. at the Credit Union 1 branch at 1310 E. Dimond Blvd. According to police, the two robbe…

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: HEARING FINDS BACKING FOR ROAD BAN The Associated Press (Published June 21, 2000) Road building should be banned in pristine areas of the Tongass National Forest, according to most of the 62 people who testified at a U.S. Forest Service hearing Monday. The hearing in the capital was the first of 13 scheduled for Southeast. The next are Thursday in Hoonah and Ketchikan. The Forest Service, acting on a directive from President Clinton, has completed a draft environmental impact statement and proposed rule that would permanently ban roads in 43 million of 54 million acres currently classified as roadless. A final EIS and rule are expected this fall. Forest Service Chief Mike Dombeck last month proposed exempting the Tongass from that rule because of a legal requirement that the agency try to …

title: Letters to the Editor
summary: ANTI-SMOKING MEASURE SAILS THROUGH ASSEMBLY By Tim Pryor (Published June 21, 2000) Starting at the end of the year, smoking will be illegal in most public places in Anchorage, the Anchorage Assembly decided Tuesday. The Assembly's 9-2 decision came after a nine-month effort by anti-smoking activists, led by the Smokefree Anchorage Coalition, who sent out e-mail, lobbied Assembly members and drew on medical experts to make their case. Some Assembly members said they had never been contacted by more people on any issue. The ban's supporters railed about the health hazards of secondhand smoke, while opponents said it would hurt business and eliminate freedom of choice. Assemblyman Allan Tesche, one of the ordinance's five sponsors, said it passed because of strong public support. About 77 per…
